generic:
Harmony University is planning a major expansion of its science and technology campus, which will include a new state-of-the-art research center. The university has partnered with BrightBuild, a leading architectural and construction firm, to oversee and manage the project. The new research facility is considered vital to helping Harmony University attract top faculty, secure grant funding, and support the growing number of students studying engineering, physics, and computer science.  

However, campus expansion projects have historically been controversial within the city. Ten years ago, the university attempted to expand student housing into a nearby neighborhood, creating backlash from community associations who felt ignored and unfairly treated in the process. Many long-term residents are still skeptical of the university’s ability to balance institutional growth with community needs.  

For this new project, the administration has emphasized the importance of repairing town-gown relations. To this end, university officials have worked closely with the local neighborhood coalition, the Riverside Residents Council, to reach agreements on a number of issues. So far, Harmony University has agreed to several important measures to address community concerns. These include:  
• Preserving a section of green space near the riverfront as a public park rather than building on it;  
• Funding the installation of new pedestrian crosswalks and bike lanes to reduce traffic risks associated with increased student and faculty commuting;  
• Partnering with local small businesses in the neighborhood to provide catering, cleaning, and landscaping contracts for the new research facility.  

Despite these positive steps, one key issue remains unresolved. The university has committed to providing a number of temporary employment opportunities during the construction phase—short-term campus project jobs intended to benefit both local residents and the broader workforce. However, BrightBuild and the Riverside Residents Council must now jointly determine:  
• The total number of temporary jobs that will be created during the project; and,  
• The share of those positions that will be reserved specifically for neighborhood residents as opposed to BrightBuild’s long-standing pool of skilled temp workers.  

The university has made it clear that at least a majority of the temporary jobs must go to Riverside residents. L. Thorne is serving as the lead project manager for BrightBuild and is representing the company in this negotiation. R. Alvarez has been selected as the spokesperson and chief negotiator for the Riverside Residents Council. Although the two have exchanged a few emails previously, this is their first in-person meeting. Both sides know that the stakes are high. The university administration has signaled that while they want the matter resolved collaboratively, they will intervene and impose their own solution if BrightBuild and the Council cannot come to terms quickly.  

For Alvarez, this negotiation is especially significant. Alvarez has been a longtime community organizer in Riverside and is widely expected to run for city council in the next election cycle. Ensuring that local residents see tangible job benefits from the project will bolster community trust and strengthen Alvarez’s political future. For Thorne, the negotiation presents an equally high-pressure situation. This is Thorne’s first opportunity to lead a university project of this scale, and BrightBuild executives are watching closely. The firm is eager to maintain its reputation for efficiency—something made more difficult because BrightBuild reportedly underbid on the project. Costs must be carefully controlled, and Thorne cannot commit to staffing arrangements that create excessive expense.

This will be a short but important negotiation for both sides. Tensions are high. There is some suspicion that BrightBuild intends to reserve the majority of temporary positions for its own established pool of skilled workers, locking out Riverside residents almost entirely. In contrast, BrightBuild has heard that Alvarez plans to demand that all temporary jobs go exclusively to neighborhood residents. If Thorne and Alvarez cannot reach agreement, university administrators have made clear that they will be very disappointed and will step in to impose their own solution unilaterally.
agent1_specific:
You are the lead project manager for BrightBuild, and this Harmony University expansion is your first chance to oversee a major high-profile academic project. Senior management within the company is closely monitoring your performance, as the firm hopes to secure more higher education contracts in the future. You are under considerable stress because the project budget is already extremely tight—BrightBuild underbid during the competition for this contract, and executives expect you to deliver results without going over cost. Any agreement that drives up expenses or complicates labor management will be seen as a failure and damage your credibility.  

The university has made clear that a majority of the construction-related temporary jobs created must go to neighborhood residents from Riverside. While you understand the political sensitivities and public relations goals behind this mandate, you are worried about the trade-offs. Many Riverside residents have little or no prior experience in skilled construction work. Your usual network of temporary workers, on the other hand, consists of trained laborers who know BrightBuild’s quality standards and methods. These workers typically give you favorable hourly rates because of their long-term relationship with the firm, and you know they generally perform more efficiently than inexperienced community hires. Your primary goal in this negotiation is to secure as many positions as possible for BrightBuild’s established pool of skilled temporary workers while still meeting the requirement that local residents make up at least a majority of hires. This will allow you to preserve efficiency, maintain quality standards, and keep subcontracted workers satisfied with continued employment. If you dramatically reduce opportunities for your regular temp workers, they may raise their rates or become less reliable for future projects—something you want to avoid at all costs.  

Your secondary goal is to minimize the total number of temporary jobs created in the first place. The fewer extra laborers you need to bring in, the lower the payroll expense and administrative burden on BrightBuild. You are willing to compromise on increasing the total headcount, but only if it substantially expands the proportion of slots you can allocate to BrightBuild’s skilled temps. Excessive job creation with too many inexperienced local residents would raise costs and slow project efficiency, which your superiors would view as unacceptable.  

To help guide you, BrightBuild executives have provided a scoring system and a minimum score threshold you must reach. For every position given to a BrightBuild temp worker, add five (5) points. For each job above a total headcount of 60, subtract three (3) points from your score. The logic from senior management is straightforward: excess hiring means higher wage costs, greater logistical oversight, and increased risk of inefficiencies. Your objective is therefore to balance the number of local hires with a sufficient block of experienced BrightBuild temps—while keeping the overall workforce lean enough to control expenses.  

In practical terms, you cannot accept any deal worth fewer than 30 total points. Falling below that threshold will be judged as an operational and financial failure that jeopardizes BrightBuild’s profitability and undermines your credibility as project lead. Moreover, you absolutely cannot commit to employing more than 90 temporary workers in total. The university’s approved site plan was designed around this ceiling: the research facility footprint, equipment staging zones, and mandatory safety clearances physically limit how many workers can operate simultaneously without creating hazardous conditions. Any figure above 90 would also breach BrightBuild’s internal headcount thresholds for a project of this contract value, triggering additional insurance and compliance costs that would push the job further into the red.  

You are aware that the Riverside Residents Council might push aggressively for 100 percent local hires to maximize community benefit. While it’s critical to avoid outright confrontation, you must make clear that a reasonable role for BrightBuild’s own temps is non-negotiable. Going forward, try to emphasize efficiency, safety, and long-term project success as selling points.
agent2_specific:
You have been chosen by the Riverside Residents Council to serve as the lead negotiator in talks with BrightBuild regarding employment opportunities during construction of Harmony University’s research facility. This appointment reflects both your years of advocacy for neighborhood interests and your growing political profile. Many within Riverside see you as the natural candidate to run for city council in the upcoming election, and this negotiation is widely viewed as a key test of your leadership. The decisions made here will have direct consequences for community livelihoods and your future credibility.  

Unemployment and underemployment remain pressing local issues. Riverside residents have endured years of neglect from large institutions seeking to expand without offering tangible benefits to those most affected. Past university projects have provided few, if any, jobs for locals—creating long-lasting resentment. This history makes the current negotiation particularly significant: residents expect to see direct hiring from their community, not empty promises.  

Your foremost priority in this negotiation is to secure as many total jobs as possible. Residents want meaningful work opportunities, not tokenistic positions. You are determined that the majority of temporary project jobs go to Riverside locals, consistent with the university’s commitment. Your second priority is to minimize the number of outside temporary workers brought in by BrightBuild. The community views this as a symbolic and practical issue: every non-resident hired is seen as a position denied to a local family in need. While you recognize BrightBuild’s interest in using its traditional pool of skilled temp workers, you must protect local opportunities first. To that end, you have quietly set a rule for yourself: under no circumstances will you accept a final agreement in which more than one-third (1/3 or 33 percent) of the total jobs are allocated to non-residents. This ceiling is both a matter of principle and political necessity, as exceeding it would be viewed as a betrayal by Riverside residents.  

At the same time, you are prepared to be flexible if a greater number of total jobs are created overall. In such cases, you may reluctantly allow more outside workers, provided the one-third limit is respected and the local share grows in absolute numbers. To guide your negotiation, you and other Council leaders have devised a scoring system to evaluate potential job distribution outcomes. This framework will help you balance total job creation with minimizing outsider involvement. For purposes of evaluation, you and the Council have agreed on a point-based scoring system. The system works as follows: for each job over 60 total, you will add five (5) points to your score, reflecting the value of maximizing opportunities for Riverside residents. However, for every outsider worker above five, you must subtract three (3) points, since additional non-resident hires are politically unpopular and undermine the Council’s goals. The higher the final score, the stronger the outcome will be for both community welfare and your political standing.  

You have also established a firm threshold: you cannot walk away from these negotiations with fewer than 45 total points. That bottom line is non-negotiable, as failing to reach it would be seen by your Council allies as a loss or capitulation. Additionally, your one-third limit remains absolute—under no scenario can you accept an agreement where outside workers constitute more than 33 percent of the jobs. This principle is crucial both substantively and symbolically.  

As you negotiate, balance flexibility with discipline. Show willingness to compromise on the margins, but never allow BrightBuild to push you below your community’s red lines. Your credibility and political future depend on it.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did L. Thorne and R. Alvarez successfully reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if L. Thorne and R. Alvarez did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they reached an agreement.

3. Based on the following conversation, if L. Thorne and R. Alvarez reached an agreement on the total number of temporary project jobs (regardless of whether they fully agreed on all terms), on what total number of temporary jobs? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.

4. Based on the following conversation, if L. Thorne and R. Alvarez reached an agreement on the number of non-resident temporary jobs (regardless of a full agreement overall on all terms), on what number of non-resident temporary jobs? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.
5. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for L. Thorne? Answer with a number.
6. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for R. Alvarez? Answer with a number.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many BrightBuild temp workers were ultimately hired?          Answer with a number between 0-90 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and (float(m.group()) * 5) / (90 * 5)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the total number of temporary jobs created?          Answer with a number between 0-90 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, 1 - max(0, float(m.group()) - 60) * 3 / (30 * 3))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the final agreement ensure that Riverside residents made up at least a majority of hires?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrightBuild successfully preserve future reliability and favorable rates from its skilled temp worker pool by ensuring them a reasonable share of jobs?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           what was the total number of temporary jobs created for the project?           Answer with a number between 0-120 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))           and max(0, min(1, (float(m.group()) - 50) / 70))       ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many non-resident (outside) workers were included in the agreement?          Answer with a number between 0-120 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, 1 - max(0, (float(m.group()) - 5) * 3 / 100))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Riverside residents receive at least a majority (over 50%) of the temporary jobs?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the final Riverside Residents Council score according to their point system?          Answer with a number or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, min(1, (float(m.group()) - 45) / 55))     ) or 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did L. Thorne and R. Alvarez reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants understand how to structure negotiations that generate value for all parties by going beyond a focus on minimum acceptable outcomes and instead creating mutually beneficial agreements. Learners will practice clarifying their own interests and best alternatives, setting ambitious but flexible goals, and actively exploring the interests of the other side to identify trade-offs across differently valued issues. They will also develop the ability to use objective standards to compare options fairly and to prevent leaving value on the table when adjusting aspirations. In addition, the case emphasizes managing the balance between value creation and relationship building, recognizing that preserving trust and long-term connections often requires careful communication, adaptability, and respect for cultural or personal differences. Finally, participants will strengthen their repertoire of negotiation approaches and reflect on when to cooperate, when to compete, and how to openly discuss relational dynamics to sustain constructive and productive negotiation partnerships.
categories: Anchoring,BATNA,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Intra-Organizational Conflict,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Principal-Agent Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: Building Trust or Building Tension: Jobs Negotiation at Harmony University Expansion
oneline: A negotiation between BrightBuild and the Riverside Residents Council to determine the total number of temporary construction jobs and the share reserved for local residents versus BrightBuild’s skilled temp workers.
